title: West Virginia found the crack in the Hughes Amendment
text: The Bottom Line West Virginia's Senate Bill 1071 proposed using a carveout written into the Hughes Amendment itself to let the state sell machine guns directly to qualified residents, legally bypassing the 1986 federal ban. The Senate President killed it before it could pass, citing drafting problems. But the legal theory behind it is sound, the crack it found in federal law is real, and that idea is not going back in the box. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. What the Hughes Amendment actually says Most people who know anything about the NFA understand the basics of the 1986 machine gun ban: the Firearm Owners' Protection Act included a last-minute amendment offered by Representative William Hughes of New Jersey that froze the civilian machine gun registry at the date of enactment, May 19, 1986. Any machine

text: Act included a last-minute amendment offered by Representative William Hughes of New Jersey that froze the civilian machine gun registry at the date of enactment, May 19, 1986. Any machine gun manufactured and registered before that date can still be transferred between civilians. Anything manufactured after that date cannot. That's the provision that turned a transferable pre-1986 M16 into a $30,000 collector's item while the functionally identical rifle in your local law enforcement armory can be purchased new for a few hundred dollars. What gets discussed far less often is the exemption language built directly into 18 U.S.C. § 922(o). The subsection that bans civilian machine gun possession explicitly does not apply to "a transfer to or by, or possession by or under the authority of, the United States or any department or agency thereof or a State, or a department, agency, or political subdivision thereof." A state, in other

text: by or under the authority of, the United States or any department or agency thereof or a State, or a department, agency, or political subdivision thereof." A state, in other words, is categorically exempted from the machine gun transfer prohibition. That's not an interpretation or a creative reading. It's the plain text. West Virginia Senate Bill 1071, introduced by Senators Chris Rose and Zack Maynard, proposed creating the Office of Public Defense within the West Virginia State Police. That office would procure machine guns, including post-1986 machine guns not available to civilians under current law, and transfer them to qualified residents who passed a background check. Because the state itself would be making the transfer, and because the state is explicitly exempted from the transfer prohibition, the federal ban arguably would not apply. Why this was more significant than it looked I'll be honest: I find it embarrassing that this legal theory sat in plain sight for four decades without anyone running a serious legislative test of it. The NFA has always been the weakest link in the legal architecture of gun control, but it has also attracted the least sustained legal attention. The major Second Amendment organizations have focused on carry rights, assault weapons bans, handgun rosters, red flag laws, and age restrictions. All of those fights are important. None of them touch the NFA. The reason is partly cultural. Most gun owners, even committed Second Amendment advocates, have internalized the post-1986 machine gun ban as a fixed reality. The

That fatalism has real consequences: it means there has been no sustained pressure on Congress or in the courts to revisit the constitutionality of the Hughes Amendment, even as Bruen and Heller fundamentally changed the analytical framework courts are supposed to apply to firearms restrictions. title: Minnesota stopped the gun control flood, but the tide is coming back
text: The Bottom Line Minnesota Democrats introduced 15 gun control bills simultaneously in early 2026, and every single one died in committee on party-line votes because the House is deadlocked 67-67 and Republicans refused to break ranks. That's the good news. The bad news is that this legislative balance is almost certain to shift after the 2026 elections, and the same playbook will come back with a functional majority behind it. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. How 15 bills become invisible There's a basic attention problem that gun rights advocates rarely talk about openly. When a state legislature introduces a single assault weapons ban, the response is predictable: organizations mobilize, emails go out, people show up at hearings, the press covers it. The bill gets scrutinized. Sometimes it gets killed. When that same legislature introduces fifteen gun

text: predictable: organizations mobilize, emails go out, people show up at hearings, the press covers it. The bill gets scrutinized. Sometimes it gets killed. When that same legislature introduces fifteen gun control measures at once, something different happens. Attention fragments. Resources spread thin. The press can't track all of it, advocates are scrambling to respond to everything, and by the time anyone figures out what matters most, some of it has already moved. This is not an accidental feature of how gun control advocates approach legislation. It is the strategy. In Minnesota's 2026 session, the package included an assault weapons ban, a magazine capacity limit of ten rounds, a ghost gun ban, a binary and reset trigger ban, a safe storage mandate, a mandatory reporting requirement, liability insurance, a 10% handgun sales tax, an 11% tax on long guns and ammunition, and several spending bills directing money toward left-wing nonprofits under

Each of those bills, presented individually, would have been a clean fight. Together, they were designed to be noise. I want to be clear about what the ammunition tax situation tells you about the intent here. The handgun tax was set at 10% and the long gun and ammunition tax was set at 11%. These were submitted as two separate bills. No rational person thinks that 1% difference reflects a policy distinction. The bills are separate because more bills means more confusion, more hearings, more committee calendars to manage, more things for gun rights advocates to respond to at the same time. The flood-the-zone strategy works when defenders are exhausted, underfunded, and surprised. It doesn't work when people recognize it for what it is.

title: Virginia's HB 217 Is an Assault Weapons Ban That Knows Exactly What It's Doing
text: The Bottom Line Virginia's HB 217 passed the House 58-34 and cleared the Senate committee stage, and Governor Spanberger has made clear she intends to sign it. The bill bans the sale and transfer of broadly defined "assault firearms" and magazines over fifteen rounds, criminalizes the purchase of the most common self-defense tools in the state, and does so through language its own sponsors cannot defend honestly in public. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. What the bill actually covers Del. Dan Helmer's HB 217 bans the importation, sale, manufacture, purchase, and transfer of "assault firearms" in Virginia, along with magazines capable of holding more than fifteen rounds. The bill's definition of "assault firearm" is where the real action is. For semi-automatic centerfire rifles, the test is a detachable magazine plus any one additional qualifying feature from

title: Virginia's HB 217 Is an Assault Weapons Ban That Knows Exactly What It's Doing
text: rounds. The bill's definition of "assault firearm" is where the real action is. For semi-automatic centerfire rifles, the test is a detachable magazine plus any one additional qualifying feature from a list that includes a folding or collapsible stock, a pistol grip, a threaded barrel, a second handgrip, or a barrel shroud. For semi-automatic centerfire pistols, the bill captures guns meeting a similar features test. The companion Senate bill SB 749 expands the pistol provisions further, and together the two bills reach an enormous swath of the Virginia firearms market. This is not a narrow restriction on one type of firearm. It is a broad features-based ban deliberately designed to capture as much as possible under a label most people associate only with AR-15s. A semi-automatic pistol with a threaded barrel for a suppressor is an "assault firearm" under this law. A rifle with a standard adjustable stock is an

text: associate only with AR-15s. A semi-automatic pistol with a threaded barrel for a suppressor is an "assault firearm" under this law. A rifle with a standard adjustable stock is an "assault firearm." Tens of millions of Virginians own firearms that would be covered, and many of them don't know it yet because nobody is calling this what it is. The language tells you everything Watching the committee hearing for this bill is instructive in the worst way. Helmer, a veteran who served in Iraq and Afghanistan, argued on the floor that "weapons similar to those I carried" should not be "trafficked" in Virginia. That framing is worth examining. The firearms covered by HB 217 are not the same as what Helmer carried. He carried select-fire military weapons capable of fully automatic fire. Civilians cannot legally own post-1986 machine guns. What HB 217 actually covers is the semi-automatic version, a mechanically

text: Helmer carried. He carried select-fire military weapons capable of fully automatic fire. Civilians cannot legally own post-1986 machine guns. What HB 217 actually covers is the semi-automatic version, a mechanically distinct firearm that fires one round per trigger pull, the same as any other semi-automatic pistol or rifle. The conflation is not accidental. Helmer's entire argument depends on the listener not understanding this distinction, or not caring about it. He invokes his military service to establish emotional credibility, implies a functional equivalence that does not exist, and then argues for a ban on weapons that are not actually what he described. More revealing was a moment in committee when a delegate used the accurate term "semi-automatic pistol," and the chairman corrected him, insisting on the term "assault pistol." What grandfathering means in practice HB 217 includes a grandfather provision for firearms lawfully owned before the effective date of July 1, 2026. You can keep what you have. You are not required to register or surrender it. That sounds like a reasonable accommodation until you read the transfer restrictions attached to it. You cannot sell a grandfathered firearm to another Virginian. You can transfer it to an immediate family member within the state, or to a buyer outside the state, and that is essentially it. When the current owners die, move out of state, or simply want to sell, the firearm either leaves Virginia permanently or cycles within a shrinking family pool. Within a generation, the civilian market for these firearms in Virginia effectively disappears without a single mandatory surrender ever being ordered. This is not accidental. This is the goal. Outright confiscation would face immediate and overwhelming political and legal resistance. Managed attrition through transfer restrictions achieves the same result over a longer timeline with less visible political cost. The constitutional landscape is genuinely unsettled. Under Bruen, laws restricting Second Amendment rights must be consistent with the historical tradition of firearm regulation at the time of the Founding. Assault weapons bans have a difficult time meeting that standard because no analogous historical regulation of widely owned semi-automatic firearms exists from the founding era. Courts applying Bruen seriously have struck down similar bans in other jurisdictions. The Heller "common use" test is also relevant here: semi-automatic rifles and standard-capacity magazines are possessed by tens of millions of Americans for lawful purposes, which under Heller should make them presumptively protected. Virginia as a warning Governor Spanberger has been in office for roughly two months, and Virginia has already passed fifteen gun control bills through the legislature. HB 217 is the most sweeping, but it is not the only one. The scope and pace of this legislative push is not the product of organic public pressure or a new evidentiary basis for these restrictions. It is the execution of a coordinated political agenda by a governor and legislative majority that have decided to move fast and force gun rights organizations to fight on every front simultaneously, knowing that no legal or political response can address fifteen bills at once. That strategy is worth

text: move fast and force gun rights organizations to fight on every front simultaneously, knowing that no legal or political response can address fifteen bills at once. That strategy is worth understanding because it will not stay in Virginia. Other states with Democratic trifectas will watch what happens here and calibrate their own legislative agendas accordingly. Virginia is the test case for how aggressively this playbook can be run, and so far the answer appears to be: very aggressively indeed. The people who pushed this bill are not confused about what it covers. They are not naive about the constitutional questions. They are moving deliberately, and anyone who cares about the Second Amendment should be paying close attention to what Virginia looks like in six months.

title: West Virginia and Kentucky Are Doing What Nobody Thought Possible on Machine Guns
text: The Bottom Line West Virginia and Kentucky have introduced legislation that uses federal law's own language to route machine gun transfers through state authority, potentially bypassing the Hughes Amendment's 1986 civilian ban. This is the most credible opening on the machine gun issue since the ban was enacted, and it creates a legal fight that could force the Supreme Court to address whether the Hughes Amendment is constitutional at all. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. A shift nobody predicted I'll be honest: I did not think I would see serious movement on the NFA and the Hughes Amendment within the next decade. What the federal law actually says The Hughes Amendment, passed as part of the Firearm Owners Protection Act of 1986, added 18 U.S.C. § 922(o) to the federal code, which made it unlawful for any person to transfer or possess a machine gun manufactured after May 19, 1986. title: Virginia's safe storage law makes self-defense a crime
text: The Bottom Line Virginia HB871 does not make anyone safer. It makes it a crime to keep a loaded firearm in your nightstand, which means it makes lawful self-defense in your own home a legal minefield. A law that punishes you for being ready to defend your family is not a safety regulation. It is an obstacle to self-preservation dressed up as one. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. What the law requires HB871 passed the Virginia House 60-34 in early March 2026, cleared the Senate with a substitute version, and is now awaiting Governor Spanberger's signature. Everything outside that narrow exception must be locked up. An unloaded firearm requires a separate storage requirement unless it is secured in a biometric safe that allows for rapid access. At first glance, this sounds reasonable. Almost nothing in gun control legislation is as it first appears. The nightstand problem The core of what is wrong with HB871 is not philosophical. title: Virginia's buyback law isn't about safety, it's about infrastructure
text: The Bottom Line Virginia's HB 702 mandates that every law enforcement agency in the state establish a gun buyback or give-back program by January 1, 2028. The research on buybacks is unambiguous: they don't reduce crime. This bill was never designed to reduce crime. It was designed to build the apparatus for something else entirely. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. What the bill actually does Virginia's HB 702 passed both chambers of the General Assembly and was headed to Governor Abigail Spanberger's desk. The bill requires every local law enforcement agency in the state to develop policies and procedures for implementing a firearm give-back or buyback program. The deadline for compliance is January 1, 2028. This is not a voluntary option for localities that want to run these programs. It is a mandate. Every sheriff's department,

Every sheriff's department, every city police force, every law enforcement agency in Virginia must have this infrastructure in place. The program's mechanics are worth understanding precisely. A person surrenders a firearm. Their identity is kept confidential, meaning the transaction is not recorded in any way that could be used against them. The firearm is then subjected to forensic testing. If it isn't connected to a crime, it is destroyed within 90 days, with a narrow exception for firearms of historical or collector value manufactured before 1945, which may be auctioned to a licensed FFL dealer. Who is against getting unwanted guns off the street? The answer to that question requires looking at what the research actually shows about these programs, and then asking a harder question about what this bill is actually for. What we know about buybacks RAND Corporation researchers, who are not a pro-gun organization, examined the evidence on gun buyback programs and found little support for the claim that they reduce firearm-related crime or death. The reasons are straightforward. People who commit crimes with guns do not voluntarily surrender them to police. The guns that get turned in at buybacks are overwhelmingly owned by older, non-violent people who have a firearm they no longer want. Those guns were not going to be used in crimes. Their removal from circulation has no effect on the people or the firearms that actually drive violence statistics. There is also a well-documented perverse incentive problem. The buyback infrastructure then exists as a mechanism for collection. I want to be careful here because I don't think HB 702 by itself constitutes confiscation. After the war, he built an academic career in aeronautical engineering, eventually teaching at Tel Aviv University and then at Virginia Tech, where he was a professor of engineering science and mechanics. On April 16, 2007, gunman Seung-Hui Cho moved through Norris Hall targeting classrooms. Librescu, 76 years old, held his classroom door shut against Cho's attempts to enter. He told his students to escape through the windows. Twenty-two of them did. Cho eventually forced his way in and shot Librescu, who died in his classroom. title: The post office carry win is bigger than it looks
text: The Bottom Line A federal judge in Texas just refused to let the Department of Justice gut a landmark ruling protecting the right to carry in post offices, and the injunction now covers all current and future members of the Firearms Policy Coalition and Second Amendment Foundation. This is commentary, not legal advice. A win worth understanding Good news in the Second Amendment space tends to get buried under the avalanche of bad news, so I want to make sure this one gets the attention it deserves. text: 17, 2026, U.S. District Chief Judge Reed O'Connor denied the Department of Justice's motion to gut the injunction in FPC v. Bondi, the case challenging the federal ban on carrying firearms in United States Post Offices. The court's September 2025 ruling had already declared the ban unconstitutional. This latest order is about scope — and the scope is broad. The DOJ had argued that injunctive relief should be limited either to the named plaintiffs or to those who were members of the Firearms Policy Coalition and the Second Amendment Foundation at the time the complaint was filed in June 2024. Judge O'Connor rejected both arguments. The injunction, he held, applies to all present and future members of both organizations. That's not a permanent guarantee — the case remains on appeal at the Fifth Circuit — but it's meaningful relief for a very large number of people in the interim. Why post offices were always an indefensible restriction Federal law has banned firearms in "federal facilities" since 1988 under 18 U.S.C. § 930. Before that, there was no general federal prohibition on carrying in government buildings, let alone post offices. The ban is a product of late-20th-century politics, not historical tradition. At the time of the founding and ratification of the Second Amendment, the handful of recognized sensitive places where carry was restricted included legislative chambers during active sessions, courtrooms while court was in session, and polling places on election day. That's the list. Post offices were not on it. Post offices in colonial America were often just private homes or taverns where mail was held for collection, and there is no historical record of treating them as disarmed zones. The "sensitive places" doctrine is, in my view, the most dangerous long-term weapon anti-gun advocates have, and they know it. title: Virginia's Assault Weapons Ban Is a Pistol Ban. Most People Don't Know That Yet.
text: The Bottom Line Virginia's SB749 is not a narrowly targeted ban on one category of firearm. It reaches everyday pistols, common shotguns, and virtually every centerfire rifle on the market through a features-based test designed to catch as much as possible under a label most people think only means AR-15s. If you own guns in Virginia and haven't read this law, you are at legal risk right now. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. The branding is the strategy Every few years the gun control movement tries a new version of the same basic move: attach a frightening label to a broad category of firearms, get the public comfortable with banning that label, and then write the actual legislation to capture as much as possible under it. Virginia's SB749, which passed the legislature in March 2026 and

title: Virginia's Assault Weapons Ban Is a Pistol Ban. Most People Don't Know That Yet.
text: public comfortable with banning that label, and then write the actual legislation to capture as much as possible under it. Virginia's SB749, which passed the legislature in March 2026 and is awaiting Governor Spanberger's signature, is the current iteration of that strategy, and it's more aggressive than anything the state has passed before. When most people hear "assault weapons ban," they picture one thing: the AR-15, or something that looks like it. Black rifle, military aesthetic, semi-automatic. The political branding around this category has been carefully cultivated over decades to make those guns seem uniquely dangerous and uniquely appropriate for restriction. And so when legislators propose banning them, a significant portion of the public nods along, because the image in their head is of something genuinely unusual. Virginia's law is built on that assumption, and then it goes much further. The bill bans not just semi-automatic centerfire rifles, but also

The bill bans not just semi-automatic centerfire rifles, but also semi-automatic centerfire pistols that meet a features test, semi-automatic shotguns that accept detachable magazines, any revolving cylinder shotgun, and any firearm with a fixed magazine capacity over 15 rounds regardless of platform. The word "assault" is doing enormous political work here. Your Ruger 22/45 doesn't look like an assault weapon to anyone. The law may reach it anyway. How the features test actually works Virginia does not use a roster system where specific models are listed and prohibited. Instead, SB749 uses a features-based approach: firearms are evaluated against a list of characteristics, and if a gun possesses a certain combination, it becomes an illegal "assault firearm." For semi-automatic centerfire rifles, you need a detachable magazine and just one additional qualifying

title: Virginia's Assault Weapons Ban Is a Pistol Ban. Most People Don't Know That Yet.
section: How the features test actually works
text: of characteristics, and if a gun possesses a certain combination, it becomes an illegal "assault firearm." For semi-automatic centerfire rifles, you need a detachable magazine and just one additional qualifying feature. The qualifying features are: a folding, telescoping, or collapsible stock; a pistol grip; a second handhold; a threaded barrel; or an attached magazine that loads from outside the pistol grip. Practically every AR-15, AK-pattern rifle, and similar firearm on the market checks those boxes. The threshold is designed to be easy to trigger. One feature is enough. For semi-automatic centerfire pistols, the bar is slightly higher: a detachable magazine plus two qualifying features from a list that includes a second handhold, a magazine outside the pistol grip (think Uzi-style), a barrel shroud, a threaded barrel, a buffer tube or arm brace, or a catch-all "characteristic of like kind" provision. That catch-all alone is troubling. Legislatures write catch-alls when they

title: Virginia's SB115 Will Turn Millions of Law-Abiding Carriers Into Criminals
text: The Bottom Line Virginia's SB115 hands a single unelected official the unchecked authority to strip millions of permit holders of their right to carry in the state, based on a legal standard that nobody bothered to define. It criminalizes people who have done everything right, and it does so deliberately, with no safety rationale offered or even attempted. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. The bill nobody is talking about Virginia's 2026 legislative session produced an avalanche of gun control. There are assault weapons bans, pistol bans, magazine restrictions, red flag expansions, and mandatory buyback provisions. Any one of those could anchor a news cycle. Senate Bill 115 is not competing for that attention, and that obscurity is precisely why it deserves scrutiny. What SB115 does is quieter, more technical, and in some respects more destructive than

section: The bill nobody is talking about
text: 115 is not competing for that attention, and that obscurity is precisely why it deserves scrutiny. What SB115 does is quieter, more technical, and in some respects more destructive than the headline bills. It dismantles Virginia's out-of-state concealed carry permit recognition almost entirely, replacing a clear universal system with a vague bureaucratic review process controlled by a single state official. Millions of people who currently carry lawfully in Virginia, including Virginia residents, will be stripped of that ability with no notice and no recourse. How recognition worked and why it mattered Before 2016, Virginia honored every valid concealed carry permit issued by any other state, provided the holder was 21 or older with a valid photo ID. No bureaucratic review. No case-by-case analysis. You had a permit, Virginia recognized it. This was the sensible default for a nation where adults routinely cross state lines. That ended temporarily in December 2015,

title: Virginia's SB115 Will Turn Millions of Law-Abiding Carriers Into Criminals
section: How recognition worked and why it mattered
text: No case-by-case analysis. You had a permit, Virginia recognized it. This was the sensible default for a nation where adults routinely cross state lines. That ended temporarily in December 2015, when then-Attorney General Mark Herring unilaterally revoked reciprocity with 25 states, citing the claim that those states had "lax" permitting standards compared to Virginia's. The backlash was immediate and severe. Six states withdrew their own reciprocity with Virginia in retaliation. The political fallout was significant enough that the arrangement was restored within roughly eight weeks. The experiment was a failure by any measure, and no crime committed by an out-of-state permit holder was cited to justify any part of it. SB115 is that same experiment, this time encoded into statute so it cannot be reversed by a single phone call from the governor's office. What the bill actually does Under SB115, the Superintendent of State Police receives authority to determine

What the bill actually does Under SB115, the Superintendent of State Police receives authority to determine whether any given state's permit system is "substantially similar" to Virginia's statutory requirements. States that fail the review lose recognition. The superintendent has until December 1, 2026, to complete that review and revoke any agreements that don't meet the threshold. The restrictions take formal effect July 1, 2027. The central problem is that "substantially similar" is not defined anywhere in the bill. There is no enumerated checklist. There is no minimum training hour threshold. There is no clear answer about whether fingerprinting requirements make a state more or less similar, or whether a higher training bar than Virginia's disqualifies a state the same way a lower one might. The superintendent can apply the standard however he chooses, and there is

The superintendent can apply the standard however he chooses, and there is no procedural mechanism for a state to challenge a negative determination. In practice, the bill contains two near-automatic disqualifiers that will gut recognition on their own. First, states must maintain a 24-hour online permit verification system or they are out. Second, permitless carry states are effectively disqualified because their permitting systems, which are often optional rather than required, cannot plausibly meet a "substantially similar" bar. There are currently 29 states that allow some form of permitless carry. Most or all of them will lose Virginia recognition automatically. The Supreme Court's ruling in New York State Rifle & Pistol Association v. Bruen confirmed that the right to carry extends beyond the home. Three decades of research say it doesn't work Gun buyback programs have been studied extensively in the United States and the evidence against their effectiveness is about as strong as social science evidence gets. The National Bureau of Economic Research examined buyback programs across 245 jurisdictions and found, at 95% confidence, they could rule out even a 1.1% reduction in firearm-related crime. A separate analysis found no evidence programs reduce suicides or homicides involving firearms. The reason is structural. Voluntary buybacks collect guns from people who are not going to commit crimes. Someone planning to shoot another person is not dropping their firearm off at a police station for a gift card. The legal logic works like this: if you can keep the gun at home but cannot carry it anywhere in public, you still technically "own" it. Legislators can claim they're not confiscating anything. Previously this kind of restriction applied only in 13 localities and only to loaded firearms. The enrolled bill removes both limitations. The rifle definitions capture most modern sporting rifles. Any semi-automatic center-fire rifle with a detachable magazine and a single qualifying feature, including a collapsible stock, pistol grip, thumbhole stock, threaded barrel, or forward grip, is prohibited. title: Pam Bondi Is Out. For Gun Owners, Todd Blanche Is the Right Call.
text: The Bottom Line Pam Bondi's time as attorney general is over, and Todd Blanche is now running the Department of Justice. For Second Amendment supporters, this is about as good an outcome as was realistically on the table. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. What actually happened On April 2, 2026, President Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi. Trump announced it matter-of-factly, praised her for her service in the administration, and named Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche as acting attorney general while the administration weighs a permanent replacement. EPA administrator Lee Zeldin has been floated as a candidate for the Senate-confirmed role. The stated reasons center on frustration over the handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files and a perceived lack of aggressiveness in pursuing the administration's priorities. Whatever the internal politics, the practical result for the gun

title: Pam Bondi Is Out. For Gun Owners, Todd Blanche Is the Right Call.

Whatever the internal politics, the practical result for the gun community is that Blanche is now running DOJ, and that matters. Bondi's record was never great on guns I want to be fair here. Bondi's DOJ was not a disaster for gun owners. The people she installed beneath her, including Blanche himself and Harmeet Dhillon at the Civil Rights Division, were genuinely committed to Second Amendment enforcement. The department's posture under Bondi improved significantly over the Biden years. Credit where it's due. But Bondi herself was never a principled Second Amendment defender, and the record in Florida makes that clear. title: Pam Bondi Is Out. For Gun Owners, Todd Blanche Is the Right Call.

She actively defended the 18-to-20 handgun purchase ban in court. Gun Owners of America flagged her mixed record publicly when she was nominated, and they were right to do so. Her confirmation hearing did not help. When pressed on gun policy, she told senators she would "enforce the laws of the land" and signaled openness to reviewing red flag proposals at the federal level. That is not the posture you want from an attorney general who sits above ATF. She was never the enemy. But there's a difference between "not the enemy" and "someone who actually believes this stuff." He attended SHOT Show 2026 in Las Vegas, met with firearm industry members on the floor, sat in on the ATF town hall, and publicly told Breitbart that anti-gun advocates are "crafty" and "creative" in their ongoing violations of the Second Amendment. He also suggested the DOJ would take additional legal action against states that continue to restrict Second Amendment rights in defiance of New York State Rifle & Pistol Association v. Bruen. title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
text: The Bottom Line Virginia HB626 strips concealed handgun permit holders of their right to carry in any building on a public college campus, converting a lawful act into a Class 1 misdemeanor punishable by up to a year in jail. One delegate defending the bill cited a Holocaust survivor's death at Virginia Tech as a reason people shouldn't be allowed to carry. That tells you everything you need to know about the intent behind this law. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. What the bill actually does Virginia House Bill 626 amends the state statute governing firearms in Commonwealth-owned buildings. Under current law, concealed handgun permit holders can carry on public university campuses unless the institution has adopted a policy restricting it. That institutional opt-out is imperfect from a rights standpoint, but it at least preserves the

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
text: can carry on public university campuses unless the institution has adopted a policy restricting it. That institutional opt-out is imperfect from a rights standpoint, but it at least preserves the legal status of carrying. HB626 eliminates the blanket higher education exemption entirely. After July 1, 2026, if this bill becomes law, your Virginia concealed handgun permit, the one the state already vetted you for, means nothing inside any campus building. Classrooms, libraries, dormitories, student centers, offices, labs: off-limits. The penalty for violation is a Class 1 misdemeanor. That's up to 12 months in jail and a substantial fine. It goes on your record and can affect your employment. Today you can lawfully carry. In three months you could go to jail for the same act. The practical impact extends well beyond students and faculty who spend most of their time on campus. Consider a physician who works in a university

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
text: jail for the same act. The practical impact extends well beyond students and faculty who spend most of their time on campus. Consider a physician who works in a university research lab. Their daily commute takes them from home, to campus, to a grocery store, to their child's school. Under HB626, that person faces an impossible choice: leave their firearm at home every day, or figure out where in their commute to safely secure it before and after campus hours. That's not a theoretical edge case. That's the routine of a lawful gun owner whose workplace happens to fall under a university's umbrella. The Liviu Librescu argument Delegate Maguire, arguing against an exception for ROTC cadets during floor debate, invoked the name of Liviu Librescu to make the case that armed resistance isn't necessary on a campus. Librescu was a Romanian-American engineering professor at Virginia Tech. He survived a Jewish

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
text: the name of Liviu Librescu to make the case that armed resistance isn't necessary on a campus. Librescu was a Romanian-American engineering professor at Virginia Tech. He survived a Jewish ghetto as a child, survived Nicolae Ceaușescu's totalitarian regime as a dissident academic, survived rejection by the Soviet bloc's academic establishment before eventually reaching the United States. On April 16, 2007, during the Virginia Tech shooting, he held the door to his classroom shut with his body while the gunman attempted to enter, allowing most of his students to escape through the windows before he was shot and killed. He was 76 years old. The shooting happened to fall on Yom HaShoah, Holocaust Remembrance Day. Delegate Maguire's argument was that because Librescu managed to protect his students without a firearm, this demonstrates that people don't need guns to fight back. I want to be precise about what this argument requires

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
text: because Librescu managed to protect his students without a firearm, this demonstrates that people don't need guns to fight back. I want to be precise about what this argument requires you to believe. It requires you to believe that a 76-year-old man dying while using his body as a door barricade represents an acceptable, even admirable, alternative to having the means to defend yourself. It requires you to celebrate an unarmed person's death as evidence that an armed person would have been unnecessary. The delegate is right that Librescu's sacrifice was heroic. He is wrong to present it as a policy argument. Heroism in a terrible situation does not prove that the situation was acceptable. Librescu was disarmed by the state's prohibition on campus carry. He did the most he could do with what he had. If he had been permitted to carry, we cannot know what would have happened,

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
text: state's prohibition on campus carry. He did the most he could do with what he had. If he had been permitted to carry, we cannot know what would have happened, but we know he would have had options beyond absorbing bullets. The claim that campus carry increases danger Proponents of bills like HB626 frequently cite the argument that more guns on campus create more risk. The research on this is genuinely contested, and I won't pretend otherwise. But a few things are clear. Virginia's CHP process is not trivial. Applicants undergo a background check, and courts verify that the applicant is not prohibited from possessing a firearm. The people who would be disarmed by HB626 are not random members of the public who stumbled into possession of a handgun. They are people the state of Virginia has already cleared to carry in grocery stores, movie theaters, shopping malls, and every

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
section: The claim that campus carry increases danger
text: the public who stumbled into possession of a handgun. They are people the state of Virginia has already cleared to carry in grocery stores, movie theaters, shopping malls, and every other public space. The argument that this same population becomes suddenly dangerous the moment they step onto a university quad requires some explanation that supporters of HB626 haven't provided. Twenty-three states currently permit campus carry in some form. The predictions of widespread violence that accompanied the expansion of campus carry laws have not materialized into a measurable pattern. That's not a definitive proof that campus carry is safe, but it does undercut the catastrophizing that drives legislation like HB626. The person most harmed by this law Something that often gets overlooked in these debates is who actually carries a concealed firearm on campus under current Virginia law. It isn't undergraduates. The typical campus carry permit holder is an adult graduate

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
section: The claim that campus carry increases danger
text: overlooked in these debates is who actually carries a concealed firearm on campus under current Virginia law. It isn't undergraduates. The typical campus carry permit holder is an adult graduate student, a faculty or staff member, or a researcher, someone with a fixed professional presence on campus who made a considered decision to carry for self-protection and went through the legal process to do so. These are not impulsive actors. They are exactly the kind of responsible gun owners the permitting process is designed to identify. HB626 tells those people that their judgment doesn't matter and their permit doesn't count as long as they're within the boundaries of a state university. It does this while doing nothing to prevent a person with violent intent from bringing a weapon onto campus in violation of the law. That's the consistent failure of gun-free zone policies: they bind the people who follow rules

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
section: The person most harmed by this law
text: person with violent intent from bringing a weapon onto campus in violation of the law. That's the consistent failure of gun-free zone policies: they bind the people who follow rules and do nothing about the people who don't. What July 1 means The bill heads to Governor Spanberger's desk with an April 13th deadline. If she signs it or simply doesn't veto it, Virginia CHP holders who carry on public college campuses face a Class 1 misdemeanor charge starting July 1st. I think most people in that situation will adjust their behavior to avoid prosecution, which is the intended outcome. The goal is not to punish gun owners. The goal is to disarm them in spaces where they would otherwise have a legal right to carry. In my view, invoking Liviu Librescu's death as a reason not to allow self-defense removed any pretense that this legislation is about safety. A

title: Virginia's Campus Carry Ban Treats Disarmament as a Feature, Not a Bug
text: have a legal right to carry. In my view, invoking Liviu Librescu's death as a reason not to allow self-defense removed any pretense that this legislation is about safety. A man survived the Holocaust, survived communist Romania, came to America, and was shot to death in a gun-free building on a Virginia campus. The lesson his story teaches about government-mandated disarmament is the opposite of the one Delegate Maguire drew from it.

title: Virginia HB21 Is a Blueprint for Killing the Gun Industry Without Banning a Single Gun
text: The Bottom Line Virginia HB21 doesn't need to ban a single gun to end your ability to buy one. It creates an open-ended liability machine that will bury gun shops, distributors, and manufacturers in litigation until they either settle into submission or close. The result is the same as an outright ban, with none of the constitutional exposure. This article is based on analysis from Bearing Freedom. Watch the original video. This is commentary, not legal advice. The lawsuit strategy the media isn't covering Virginia's 2026 legislative session sent roughly 18 gun control bills to Governor Abigail Spanberger's desk. Most of the coverage has gone to the assault weapons ban, the magazine limits, the carry restrictions. Those are real threats and they deserve scrutiny. But House Bill 21, which received a fraction of that attention, is in my view the most structurally dangerous piece of legislation in the entire package.

title: Virginia HB21 Is a Blueprint for Killing the Gun Industry Without Banning a Single Gun
section: The lawsuit strategy the media isn't covering
text: and they deserve scrutiny. But House Bill 21, which received a fraction of that attention, is in my view the most structurally dangerous piece of legislation in the entire package. HB21 creates a new civil liability framework for what it calls "firearm industry members." That definition is broad: gun manufacturers, yes, but also retailers, distributors, and sellers of firearm-related products, which the bill extends to ammunition, components, unfinished receivers, and any accessory that alters or enhances firing capability. Under some readings, that could include a holster. The practical scope of the bill is essentially everything connected to the lawful firearms market. The bill empowers three categories of plaintiffs to sue: the state attorney general, any local prosecutor or city attorney in Virginia, and private individuals who can claim injury from a violation. That last category is particularly significant. Bloomberg-aligned organizations have deep financial resources and a demonstrated willingness to fund

title: Virginia HB21 Is a Blueprint for Killing the Gun Industry Without Banning a Single Gun
section: The lawsuit strategy the media isn't covering
text: in Virginia, and private individuals who can claim injury from a violation. That last category is particularly significant. Bloomberg-aligned organizations have deep financial resources and a demonstrated willingness to fund litigation campaigns. Under HB21, any private plaintiff can seek compensatory damages, punitive damages, injunctions, and attorney fees. The litigation doesn't have to succeed. The cost of defending it is the point. The federal protection they're trying to sidestep Congress passed the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act in 2005 specifically to block this kind of lawsuit. The PLCAA prevents civil actions against firearms manufacturers and dealers when their products are later used criminally by third parties. The logic is sound: a gun shop that runs a background check and completes a legal transfer has no more responsibility for what a buyer does years later than a car dealer has for a drunk driver. HB21 attempts to route around that

title: Virginia HB21 Is a Blueprint for Killing the Gun Industry Without Banning a Single Gun
section: The federal protection they're trying to sidestep
text: completes a legal transfer has no more responsibility for what a buyer does years later than a car dealer has for a drunk driver. HB21 attempts to route around that protection by creating new state-level duties that don't exist under federal law. Virginia is not alone. At least ten other states have enacted similar "firearm industry responsibility" statutes, and anti-gun advocates have been explicit that this is a coordinated strategy. When you can't win on outright bans, you make lawful commerce economically impossible. The NRA-ILA has called these laws an existential threat to the industry, and I think that's accurate. "Reasonable controls" with no fixed definition The mechanism HB21 uses is particularly well designed for harassment. It requires every firearm industry member to implement "reasonable controls" to prevent sales to straw purchasers, prohibited persons, and anyone the seller has "reasonable cause to believe" poses a substantial risk of harm. It

section: The federal protection they're trying to sidestep
text: industry member to implement "reasonable controls" to prevent sales to straw purchasers, prohibited persons, and anyone the seller has "reasonable cause to believe" poses a substantial risk of harm. It also requires safeguards against theft, prevention of auto sear installation, and compliance with existing consumer protection law. The immediate response should be: FFLs in Virginia are already running NICS background checks. They're already legally prohibited from knowingly selling to prohibited persons. The "reasonable controls" requirement doesn't add meaningful safety obligations on top of what existing federal law already demands. What it adds is a private right of action when those obligations are allegedly violated, with liability standards defined not by statute but by whoever is suing you, after the fact. That deliberate vagueness is not an oversight. What counts as sufficient theft prevention? A padlock? A vault? Armed guards? HB21 does not say. The bill cannot say, because the entire

text: That deliberate vagueness is not an oversight. What counts as sufficient theft prevention? A padlock? A vault? Armed guards? HB21 does not say. The bill cannot say, because the entire utility of the law depends on that ambiguity. A small FFL with 200 square feet and one employee cannot possibly know in advance whether their security setup satisfies the standard that will be applied against them in litigation. That uncertainty alone will drive dealers out of business. Bedford County Sheriff Mike Miller made the obvious point when the bill was being debated: the guns used in crimes generally don't come from lawful retail transactions. They come from the black market and illegal purchases. HB21 does nothing to address that pipeline. It only imposes costs on people who are already following the law. What this looks like in practice Consider a scenario the bill explicitly enables. Someone purchases a firearm legally

Later, that person sells the gun privately, and it ends up used in a crime. Under HB21's framework, a private plaintiff, potentially funded by a national gun control organization, could argue the dealer failed to implement "reasonable controls" to prevent downstream diversion and file a civil suit seeking punitive damages and injunctive relief. The dealer may have done absolutely nothing wrong. But defending a civil suit in Virginia costs money that most small FFLs do not have. The outcome most dealers will face is not a verdict, it's a settlement that imposes court-supervised "controls" the legislature never actually voted on. That is de facto gun

Gun shop owner Mark Moorefield, who testified against the bill, put it plainly: a scenario where he lawfully sold a firearm that later left his customer's possession through a private transfer could still expose him to liability. There is no sensible legal system in which that outcome is just. The end state Virginia's gun control package this session is not a collection of independent policy choices. It's a coordinated effort to reach a disarmed civilian population through multiple vectors simultaneously. The assault weapons ban addresses what you can buy. The carry bans address where you can take it. HB21 addresses whether the stores that sell it will still exist. If HB21 functions as designed, Virginia's licensed

If HB21 functions as designed, Virginia's licensed dealers face waves of litigation from a hostile attorney general, local prosecutors with political incentives, and well-funded private organizations pursuing a predetermined goal. Many will close or stop selling in the state. Manufacturers may decide the legal exposure isn't worth doing business in Virginia at all. The Second Amendment right becomes technically intact but practically unexercisable. I think the Second Amendment community sometimes underestimates how serious this approach is because it lacks the visual drama of a confiscation order. But the Constitution guarantees the right to keep and bear arms. A regime that makes it impossible to acquire arms achieves the same result as an outright ban, just with better legal cover.
